title: Getinge Servo-i Ventilator Expiratory Cassette Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting expiratory cassette errors on a Servo-i ventilator, focusing on external causes before repair.

steps.title: Ensure the ventilator is powered off before handling the cassette
steps.instructions: Safety first: avoid disconnecting tubing or cassettes while the device is active on a patient.

steps.title: Verify expiratory cassette installation
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the expiratory cassette. Listen for the “click” indicating it is fully seated. Misalignment can cause error codes.

steps.title: Inspect cassette and sensors for debris or damage
steps.instructions: Check for moisture, secretions, or obstructions in the expiratory cassette. Ensure the sensor ports are clean and unobstructed.

steps.title: Check expiratory tubing connections
steps.instructions: Confirm all patient circuit tubing is securely connected. Ensure there are no kinks, pinches, or leaks that could trigger cassette errors.

steps.title: Run a ventilator self-test
steps.instructions: After verifying installation and connections, power on the ventilator and initiate a system self-test. Observe whether the expiratory cassette error clears. If the error persists, note the exact error code for escalation.

steps.title: Verify software and calibration status
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator’s software is up to date. Confirm calibration logs do not indicate failed expiratory cassette calibration.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displays expiratory cassette error code during setup.
CCR.Cause: Cassette was not fully seated; tubing connections were loose.
CCR.Resolution: Reinstalled expiratory cassette, secured tubing connections, ran self-test—device passed and returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Tubing checked for leaks or kinks
Final device status: Ready for patient use or Out of Service

title: Getinge Servo-i Ventilator - Flow Sensor or Volume Measurement Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow sensor errors and tidal volume discrepancies on Getinge Servo-i ventilators, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Verify Power and Startup Sequence
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is properly powered and the battery is charged. Check that the ventilator completes its self-test without unexpected alarms. Why: Incomplete self-tests or unstable power can trigger false flow errors.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure inspiratory and expiratory limbs are correctly connected. Confirm that filters, humidifiers, and heat moisture exchangers (HMEs) are properly seated. Why: Leaks or misaligned tubing can cause erroneous volume readings.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor (Pneumotach) and Adapter
steps.instructions: Remove and visually inspect the flow sensor for condensation, debris, or damage. Ensure the sensor is installed in the correct orientation according to the device labeling. Why: Obstructions, moisture, or incorrect installation can trigger measurement errors.

steps.title: Inspect Circuit for Leaks
steps.instructions: Perform a leak test if the ventilator supports it. Check all connections, including endotracheal tube cuff seal, Y-piece, and filters. Why: Leaks affect tidal volume delivery and flow measurement accuracy.

steps.title: Check Humidification and Condensation
steps.instructions: Drain excess water from heated circuits or HMEs. Confirm that the flow sensor is dry. Why: Condensation in the sensor or tubing alters flow and volume readings.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Consumables
steps.instructions: Replace the flow sensor or adapter with a verified functioning unit. Replace disposable patient circuit if necessary. Why: Confirms whether the error is due to consumable components rather than the ventilator hardware.

steps.title: Verify Settings and Calibration
steps.instructions: Confirm ventilator mode, tidal volume, and circuit type are correctly configured. Re-run calibration or zeroing procedures as per normal operating procedure. Why: Incorrect configuration can cause alarms for “flow error” or “volume discrepancy.”

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displays “flow sensor error” and delivered tidal volume inconsistent.
CCR.Cause: External inspection revealed condensation in flow sensor and minor leak at Y-piece connection.
CCR.Resolution: Drained condensation, secured connections, replaced disposable sensor and patient circuit, recalibrated ventilator. Verified proper tidal volume delivery.

title: Getinge Servo-i Ventilator - Fails Pre-Use Check / 'Leak Detected'
description: Guide for troubleshooting a Getinge Servo-i ventilator that fails pre-use check with a 'Leak Detected' message, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Ensure Device Is Powered and Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator is plugged into a functional outlet. Check that the battery (if present) is charged and the unit is powered on. Why: Low or unstable power can cause false leak detection.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Ensure all tubing (inspiratory and expiratory limbs) is correctly connected. Confirm that tubing is free of kinks, cracks, or holes. Remove and reconnect tubing to ensure secure connections. Why: Even a small disconnection or crack can trigger a leak alarm.

steps.title: Check the Patient Interface
steps.instructions: Inspect the endotracheal tube, mask, or other patient connector. Ensure the device is properly attached with no loose connections. Why: Leaks often occur at the interface rather than inside the ventilator.

steps.title: Inspect the Exhalation Valve / Humidifier
steps.instructions: Remove or check the exhalation valve for proper seating. If a heated humidifier is attached, confirm all connections are tight and tubing is intact. Why: A misaligned valve or humidifier connection can mimic a leak.

steps.title: Verify the Circuit Type Selected in Software
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator software settings match the connected circuit (adult, pediatric, neonatal). Why: Mismatched settings can cause pre-use check failures.

steps.title: Perform a Manual Leak Test
steps.instructions: Disconnect from the patient. Close the circuit as recommended in the user interface. Run the ventilator’s 'circuit check' or leak test again. Why: Confirms whether the leak is in the patient circuit or the ventilator.

steps.title: Replace Consumable Components if Needed
steps.instructions: Swap out disposable circuits or valves with known-good units. Why: Consumables are common sources of leaks and are easily replaced without opening the ventilator.

CCR.Complaint: Pre-use check fails with ‘Leak Detected’ message.
CCR.Cause: Observed loose circuit connection at inspiratory limb. No cracks in tubing, valves seated correctly, no patient connected. Leak persisted after reconnecting.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced disposable breathing circuit with a verified functional circuit. Pre-use check passed successfully. Ventilator returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Functional, returned to service

title: Getinge Servo-u Ventilator – Expiratory Cassette Flow Sensor Failure or Calibration Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting expiratory cassette flow sensor failures and calibration errors on the Getinge Servo-u ventilator, emphasizing external checks before internal failure.

steps.title: Remove the Patient from the Ventilator (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Transition the patient to another ventilator immediately. Do not troubleshoot while connected to a patient. This protects patient safety and prevents ventilation interruption during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Alarm or Error Message
steps.instructions: Document the specific message displayed: “Expiratory cassette calibration failed”, “Flow sensor error”, “Expiratory measurement fault”, Failed system/pre-use check. Precise wording matters and should be included in the work order.

steps.title: Inspect the Expiratory Cassette
steps.instructions: Remove the cassette and visually inspect for cracks in housing, warped plastic, damaged seals or gaskets, and obvious contamination. If physically damaged — replace the cassette. Why this matters: Improper sealing or damage directly affects flow measurement accuracy.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Condensation
steps.instructions: Inspect for water pooling, condensation inside the cassette, or secretions in the expiratory limb. Moisture is a common cause of flow calibration errors. If moisture is present: replace with a dry cassette, replace or dry the expiratory limb, confirm heated circuits are functioning properly, do not attempt to open or internally dry the cassette.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Cassette Seating
steps.instructions: Reinsert the cassette carefully. Ensure it clicks fully into place, confirm latch engagement, and check for debris in the cassette slot. Even slight misalignment can cause calibration failures.

steps.title: Run a New Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: After reseating or replacing the cassette, perform a full ventilator system check and observe whether calibration completes successfully. If calibration now passes, the issue was likely moisture, seating, or cassette condition.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Cassette
steps.instructions: If errors persist, install a known-good expiratory cassette and run calibration again. If the new cassette resolves the issue, the original cassette is faulty and should be removed from service. If the new cassette also fails, suspect internal measurement module or interface issue.

steps.title: Inspect Cassette Interface Area
steps.instructions: Without disassembly, look inside the cassette compartment, check for debris, inspect electrical contact points (no cleaning with liquids), and ensure no bent pins or obvious physical damage. If internal contact damage is suspected — escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator failed pre-use check with “Expiratory cassette calibration failed” error. Unit unavailable for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Moisture observed inside expiratory cassette. Calibration failed repeatedly until cassette replaced.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced expiratory cassette with known-good unit. Pre-use check passed. Ventilator returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurred during patient use

title: Getinge Servo-u Ventilator – Software Communication Faults Between Main Board and Modules
description: Guide for troubleshooting software communication errors between the main board and internal modules of the Getinge Servo-u ventilator, focusing on external checks before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure the Patient Is Safely Supported
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting: Confirm the ventilator is not actively supporting a patient. If it is, transition the patient to another ventilator immediately. Communication faults can escalate into full ventilator shutdown.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the ventilator off using the proper shutdown procedure. Disconnect AC power. Wait at least 60–90 seconds. Reconnect AC power and power the unit back on. Software communication faults are often caused by temporary logic lockups or corrupted module handshakes during boot. Observe: Does the system complete boot? Does the same communication error return immediately? If the fault clears and does not return, monitor the device for recurrence.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Integrity
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is tested and grounded. Try a known-good outlet. Remove from extension cords or power strips. Confirm no recent electrical events occurred (outage, generator test, brownout). Voltage instability can disrupt internal communication buses between boards.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify battery charge level on startup. If battery is critically low, allow full charging. If battery warnings are present, note them. A failing battery or unstable DC bus voltage can create intermittent communication errors between internal modules.

steps.title: Inspect External Module Connections (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: If the ventilator has externally accessible modules or option boards: Power off the unit. Inspect accessible connectors for loose seating, bent pins, debris. Do not open internal housings. If a module appears partially seated, carefully reseat it. Poor connection integrity can interrupt communication between the main board and subsystems.

steps.title: Observe Boot Behavior Carefully
steps.instructions: During startup: Does the error occur immediately? After self-test? Does a specific module name appear in the error? Document exactly what is displayed. This helps determine if a specific module is failing, the main control board is failing, or a communication bus is unstable.

steps.title: Run Full Pre-Use/System Check
steps.instructions: If the ventilator boots: Perform a complete pre-use check. Monitor for module detection failures, test aborts, intermittent system freezes. If the unit cannot pass pre-use testing, it should not return to service.

steps.title: Assess for Environmental Contributors
steps.instructions: Check for excessive heat exposure, recent cleaning fluid intrusion, visible signs of liquid damage, strong chemical odor, fan not running or unusual noise. Moisture or contamination can affect communication circuits.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displaying communication fault between control unit and gas module. Fails startup self-test.
CCR.Cause: Unit powered on and displayed persistent internal module communication error. Fault remained after controlled power cycle and verified AC source. Pre-use test failed at module detection phase.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service. Labeled Out of Service. Sent to manufacturer-authorized repair for internal board evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether error occurs immediately or intermittently

title: Getinge Servo-u Ventilator – Gas Supply Pressure Alarms Despite Adequate Wall Supply
description: Troubleshooting guide for Servo-u ventilator gas supply pressure alarms when wall supply appears normal.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is stable. If the ventilator is actively alarming and ventilation is compromised, immediately transition the patient to another ventilator or manual ventilation before troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot gas supply issues on an actively dependent patient.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Gas Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct gas outlets are being used (Air to Air, O₂ to O₂). Check that the wall outlets are firmly engaged and not partially disconnected. Test the outlet using a known-good device or a pressure gauge if available. Even if other equipment is working, the specific outlet feeding this ventilator may have intermittent pressure fluctuation.

steps.title: Check Gas Hoses
steps.instructions: Inspect both Air and O₂ hoses from wall to ventilator. Look for kinks, compression under bed wheels, cracks or worn fittings, and loose DISS or quick-connect fittings. Swap with known-good hoses to rule out internal hose obstruction. A partially obstructed hose can create pressure drop under flow demand, triggering alarms despite static wall pressure appearing normal.

steps.title: Confirm Secure Connection at the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Ensure both gas fittings are fully seated and hand-tightened (if DISS type). Listen for leaks. Check for hissing or audible flow irregularities. Loose fittings may pass static pressure but fail under ventilator demand.

steps.title: Inspect Water Traps and Filters (If Installed)
steps.instructions: Check for moisture accumulation, blockage, or incorrect installation direction in any inline water traps or filters. Replace or remove temporarily for testing if obstruction is suspected.

steps.title: Review Alarm Message Details
steps.instructions: Access the ventilator alarm log and note which gas source is alarming (Air, O₂, or both), whether it is low pressure, high pressure, or fluctuation alarm, and timing (continuous vs intermittent). If both gases are alarming simultaneously, suspect internal pneumatic module issue, internal pressure sensor fault, or software-related monitoring fault. If only one gas alarms, focus troubleshooting on that specific supply line.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator (When Safe)
steps.instructions: After patient transfer or safe condition, power down fully, disconnect AC power briefly, and restart the system. Transient sensor faults can occasionally clear after reboot.

steps.title: Run a Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Perform the full system pre-use check and observe supply pressure readings, calibration results, and leak test outcome. Failure during the supply pressure verification phase strongly suggests internal sensing or pneumatic regulation issues.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarming low O₂ supply pressure despite wall outlet showing normal pressure.
CCR.Cause: Observed persistent low O₂ supply alarm after confirming adequate wall pressure and replacing gas hose. Pre-use check failed supply pressure verification.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service. Labeled Out of Service and sent to vendor for pneumatic module evaluation.

title: Getinge Servo-u Ventilator – Internal Battery Not Charging or Reduced Runtime
description: Troubleshooting guide for Getinge Servo-u ventilator internal battery not charging or reduced runtime, focusing on external power verification and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure the Patient Is Safe First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is stable and connected to wall power. If evaluating runtime concerns, move the patient to another ventilator before testing battery performance. Battery troubleshooting should never compromise ventilation.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is fully plugged into a verified hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with a known-good device or plug into a different outlet. Check for tripped breakers or power strips (if used temporarily). If the ventilator is not receiving consistent AC power, the battery will not charge.

steps.title: Confirm Power Cord and Connection Integrity
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC power cord for damage, cuts, bent prongs, or loose connections. Ensure the cord is fully seated at the ventilator power inlet. Swap with a known-good power cord if available. A loose or damaged cord is a common and easily overlooked cause.

steps.title: Check On-Screen Battery Status Indicators
steps.instructions: Review battery charge level percentage. Check for active battery alarms. Confirm the system indicates “Charging” when plugged in. If the display does not indicate charging while confirmed on AC power, further evaluation is needed.

steps.title: Review Alarm History
steps.instructions: Access the alarm/event log and look for repeated power interruption events, battery fault messages, and charger-related alarms. Frequent power interruptions may explain reduced runtime due to incomplete charging cycles.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure ventilation openings are not blocked. Check for excessive dust buildup. Confirm the ventilator is not placed against a wall restricting airflow. Overheating can reduce battery life and charging efficiency.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Runtime Check (Bench Test Only)
steps.instructions: Do not perform on an actively ventilated patient. Fully charge the ventilator while powered off or idle. Disconnect from AC power. Measure runtime under test lung conditions. Compare runtime to expected performance. Significantly reduced runtime suggests battery degradation.

steps.title: Check for Recent Preventive Maintenance
steps.instructions: Verify last PM date. Confirm battery age. Review if battery replacement interval has been exceeded. Internal batteries have a limited service life and degrade over time.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displayed low battery alarm and powered down during transport despite being plugged in earlier.
CCR.Cause: Unit confirmed receiving AC power. Power cord and outlet verified functional. Battery indicated 100% but runtime test lasted only 8 minutes, indicating degraded internal battery.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service. Labeled Out of Service. Sent to manufacturer-authorized repair for battery replacement and charger system evaluation.

title: Getinge Servo-u Ventilator – User Interface Touchscreen or Rotary Knob Input Faults
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive touchscreen or rotary knob input issues on the Getinge Servo-u ventilator, focusing on external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure the Patient Is Safe First
steps.instructions: If the ventilator is actively in use: Transfer the patient to another ventilator if possible. Do not troubleshoot a compromised interface on an active patient unless absolutely necessary. Confirm ventilation is stable before proceeding. Patient safety is always the priority.

steps.title: Check for Screen Lock or Standby Mode
steps.instructions: Verify the screen is not in screen lock mode. Confirm the unit is not in standby or alarm silence state affecting user input. Attempt to wake the display by touching multiple screen areas. Why this matters: Some “unresponsive” complaints are simply locked interface states.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: If clinically safe: Place the ventilator in standby. Power down fully using the proper shutdown sequence. Disconnect AC power briefly (30–60 seconds). Reconnect and power up. Observe: Does the screen respond normally after reboot? Does the rotary knob function during startup menus? Why this matters: Temporary UI processor faults can resolve with a clean reboot.

steps.title: Inspect AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify AC power indicator is illuminated. Check for battery alarms or unstable power messages. Test outlet with another device if needed. Inspect power cord for damage. Low voltage or unstable power can cause erratic touchscreen behavior. Document: Outlet tested. Power cord condition. Battery icon status.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Check for moisture, cleaning residue, or dried disinfectant film. Inspect for cracks or physical damage. Confirm no plastic overlays or protective films are interfering. Clean using approved wipes and allow to dry fully. Why this matters: Capacitive touchscreens can misread input if contaminated.

steps.title: Evaluate Rotary Knob Function Separately
steps.instructions: Rotate knob slowly and observe screen response. Press knob to confirm selection registers. Listen for mechanical clicking feedback. Check for physical looseness or sticking. If: Touchscreen works but knob does not → likely rotary encoder issue. Knob works but touchscreen does not → likely display digitizer issue. Neither works → possible UI control board or software fault.

steps.title: Check for Alarm or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review system messages for: UI communication errors. Display errors. System bus faults. Software version anomalies. Document any error codes shown.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: If accessible without disassembly: Verify display cable connections are secure (if external). Check for recent service activity. Inspect rear connectors for looseness or damage. Do not open internal panels unless authorized for service-level repair.

steps.title: Run System Self-Test (If Available)
steps.instructions: If the ventilator allows: Perform a system check or extended self-test. Observe whether interface faults are detected. If the unit fails self-test related to UI components, further escalation is appropriate.

CCR.Complaint: “Touchscreen not responding to touch; unable to change ventilation settings.”
CCR.Cause: “Touchscreen intermittently unresponsive after power cycle. Rotary knob functional. Screen clean and undamaged. No power issues identified. Suspected touchscreen digitizer failure.”
CCR.Resolution: “Ventilator removed from service. Labeled Out of Service. Sent to vendor for touchscreen assembly evaluation and repair.”

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Removed from service / Returned to service)

title: Getinge GSS Series Washer-Disinfector - Cycle Abort Due to Insufficient Water Pressure
description: Troubleshoot cycle aborts triggered by low water pressure in Getinge GSS Series washer-disinfectors, focusing on external verifiable checks before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not run patient instrument cycles while troubleshooting water issues. Move critical instruments to an alternative washer-disinfector if available. Inform staff of Out-of-Service status.

steps.title: Verify Water Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure the facility’s water supply valves to the washer-disinfector are fully open. Check that supply lines are not kinked, blocked, or leaking. Low inlet pressure is the most common cause of aborts.

steps.title: Check Water Pressure
steps.instructions: Use a calibrated pressure gauge at the inlet to confirm water pressure meets the manufacturer’s minimum requirement (usually around 20–40 psi, depending on model). Pressure below specification will trigger automatic cycle aborts.

steps.title: Inspect Filters and Strainers
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect any inlet filters or strainers for debris or scale buildup. Clean or replace as needed. Clogged filters reduce flow and can cause low-pressure alarms.

steps.title: Confirm Water Temperature Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure hot/cold supply lines are within proper temperature ranges for cycles requiring pre-tempered water. Temperature sensors can affect flow validation and trigger aborts.

steps.title: Examine Hoses and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for leaks, loose fittings, or partial obstructions in the supply lines and internal connections. Even minor restrictions can reduce effective pressure.

steps.title: Observe Pump Operation
steps.instructions: Listen for unusual sounds or vibrations from the internal water pump during cycle start. Pump failure or cavitation can mimic low-pressure conditions.

steps.title: Cycle Restart Attempt
steps.instructions: After addressing external factors, attempt a test cycle with empty or minimal load to verify water pressure is sufficient.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector aborts cycle with “Insufficient Water Pressure” alarm.
CCR.Cause: Inlet valve partially closed, filter clogged, or supply line kinked; verified by observation and pressure reading.
CCR.Resolution: Fully opened supply valve, cleaned filter, cleared kinked hoses, restarted test cycle successfully.

title: Getinge GSS Series Washer-Disinfector - Detergent Dosing Pump Failure
description: Troubleshooting detergent dosing pump failures on Getinge GSS Series washer-disinfectors with external checks before assuming internal pump failure.

steps.title: Verify Device Power and Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the washer-disinfector is powered on and not in a fault state. Check the main display for any active alarms. The dosing pump may not operate if the unit detects other faults.

steps.title: Check Detergent Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm the detergent container is present, filled, and properly seated. Inspect tubing for kinks, blockages, or disconnections. An empty container or blocked line can trigger a pump error.

steps.title: Inspect Pump Connections and Electrical Supply
steps.instructions: Verify that the dosing pump is securely connected to the washer-disinfector. Check for loose connectors, damaged wires, or tripped circuit breakers. Electrical issues prevent the pump from operating.

steps.title: Run a Manual Pump Test (if available)
steps.instructions: Some models allow manual activation of the dosing pump via the control panel. Observe if detergent is delivered properly. Confirms pump functionality independently of a full cycle.

steps.title: Check for Alarm History or Error Codes
steps.instructions: Review the system log for any specific dosing pump errors or codes. Reference the manufacturer’s error guide for code interpretation. Identifies if the issue is recurring or intermittent.

steps.title: Inspect Pump Mechanically
steps.instructions: Without disassembly, check for obvious external damage, leaks, or unusual resistance when turning the pump shaft by hand (if safe). Mechanical blockage or external damage can prevent operation.

CCR.Complaint: Detergent dosing pump failed; alarm displayed during cycle, no detergent delivered.
CCR.Cause: Pump tubing and container were intact, electrical connections verified, pump does not actuate manually; internal pump failure suspected.
CCR.Resolution: Washer-disinfector labeled Out of Service and sent for bench repair. Alternate washer used for patient instrument cleaning.

helpfulDetails: Observation of pump during manual test

title: Getinge GSS Series Washer-Disinfector - Spray Arm Blockage Causing Poor Cleaning Alarms
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot spray arm blockages causing poor cleaning alarms on Getinge GSS Series washer-disinfectors.

steps.title: Check Cycle and Alarm Messages
steps.instructions: Review the alarm display and cycle log for “poor cleaning” or related messages. Confirm whether this occurs on a single cycle or multiple load types. Why: Confirms the issue is consistent and not a one-time anomaly.

steps.title: Verify Detergent and Water Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure detergent is present and dosing correctly. Confirm incoming water supply pressure and temperature meet specifications. Why: Inadequate water or detergent can mimic spray arm blockages.

steps.title: Inspect Spray Arms Visually
steps.instructions: Open the chamber and remove or lift spray arms. Check for obvious debris, hard water deposits, or kinks. Why: Blockages in spray nozzles are often visible and easy to remove.

steps.title: Manually Clear Obstructions
steps.instructions: Remove visible debris using soft brushes or approved cleaning tools. Ensure spray arm rotation is free and unobstructed. Why: Freely moving spray arms are essential for uniform water coverage.

steps.title: Check Spray Arm Rotation Mechanism
steps.instructions: Spin the arms by hand to confirm they rotate smoothly. Look for damaged or loose mounts that might hinder movement. Why: Even clean arms won’t distribute water effectively if they cannot rotate.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle
steps.instructions: Perform a rinse cycle without instruments and observe spray coverage. Verify alarms clear and water reaches all parts of the chamber. Why: Confirms that external cleaning steps resolved the blockage.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector alarms indicate poor cleaning; visual inspection shows inconsistent spray coverage.
CCR.Cause: Spray arms partially blocked with debris and mineral deposits; rotation hindered.
CCR.Resolution: Removed spray arms, cleared obstructions, verified rotation, and ran test cycle confirming proper coverage; alarms cleared.

helpfulDetails: Checked water inlet pressure and temperature
Confirmed detergent presence and proper dosing
Observed spray arm rotation after cleaning
Documented any unusual noises or resistance during arm rotation
Final device status: operational with proper spray coverage

title: Getinge GSS Series Washer-Disinfector - Heating Failure Preventing Disinfection Temperature
description: Guide for troubleshooting situations where the GSS Series washer-disinfector fails to reach the required disinfection temperature.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Only troubleshoot the heater when the chamber is empty and the device is off. Do not attempt disinfection on active patient instruments if temperature cannot be verified.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check the device is plugged into a dedicated outlet with proper voltage and grounding. Ensure no tripped breakers or blown fuses are present. Why: Heater elements require full power; low or unstable voltage can prevent heating.

steps.title: Check Water Supply and Inlet Temperature
steps.instructions: Ensure the inlet water valve is open and supply pressure is within specifications. Verify incoming water temperature is not excessively low. Why: Some washer-disinfectors adjust heater load based on inlet water temperature; extremely cold water can delay or prevent reaching setpoint.

steps.title: Inspect for Water Flow Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check filters, strainers, and inlet hoses for clogs. Ensure spray arms move freely and no debris is blocking circulation. Why: Poor circulation reduces heat transfer, causing slow or incomplete heating.

steps.title: Confirm Cycle and Program Settings
steps.instructions: Verify the selected program requires thermal disinfection and is not a cold or pre-rinse cycle. Check that any optional “eco” or “low temp” modes are not active. Why: Incorrect program selection may prevent heaters from activating fully.

steps.title: Check Heater Indicators and Alarms
steps.instructions: Observe any heater fault indicators or error codes on the display. Document exact messages for work order and potential vendor support. Why: Built-in diagnostics can help determine whether the heater circuit or control system is at fault.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: Check terminal connections to the heater assembly for looseness or corrosion. Why: Poor connections can prevent power delivery to heaters.

CCR.Complaint: User reports that the washer-disinfector does not reach disinfection temperature and cycle alarms for ‘temperature not reached.’
CCR.Cause: Verified power supply, water supply, circulation, and program selection. Heater failed to reach setpoint; no external faults identified.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair to vendor service.

title: Getinge GSS Series Washer-Disinfector - Door Lock or Door Seal Leak Faults
description: Guide for troubleshooting door lock or seal faults on Getinge GSS Series washer-disinfectors, emphasizing external checks and safety.

steps.title: Verify Device Power and Ready Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the washer-disinfector is powered on and in standby mode. Check that the control panel shows “ready” and no prior fault messages persist. Door interlock sensors may not register if the system is not properly powered.

steps.title: Inspect Door Seal (Gasket) for Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the silicone or rubber door seal for cracks, tears, foreign objects, or residue buildup. Clean gently with a manufacturer-approved cleaning solution if residue is present. Even small debris or damage can prevent a proper seal, triggering leak faults.

steps.title: Check Door Lock Mechanism
steps.instructions: Ensure the door closes fully without resistance. Observe the mechanical lock or latch as it engages. Verify that the door lock indicator on the panel lights appropriately. Faulty door lock sensors or mechanical interference can prevent the cycle from starting.

steps.title: Inspect Door Hinges and Alignment
steps.instructions: Check that the door sits evenly and that hinges are not bent or obstructed. Adjust alignment if allowed by manufacturer guidance. Misalignment can prevent proper sealing and engagement of interlock switches.

steps.title: Check for Water or Residue on Door Frame
steps.instructions: Wipe the frame and the inner perimeter of the door. Ensure no water pooling or chemical residues are present. Residues can compromise the seal and trigger leak detection alarms.

steps.title: Run a Door Test (Empty Cycle)
steps.instructions: If the system allows, run a short diagnostic or “door test” cycle without load. Observe if the system still reports a leak or door lock fault. Confirms if the issue persists under controlled conditions before escalating.

CCR.Complaint: User reported alarms stating “door not closed” or “leak detected.”
CCR.Cause: Observed minor residue on door seal and slightly misaligned door hinges preventing full engagement of the door lock.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned door seal, wiped door frame, and adjusted door alignment. Ran empty cycle successfully; alarms cleared. Device returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Leak alarms observed: Initial fault present
Final device status: Returned to service after verification

title: Getinge Magnus Series Surgical Table - Table Will Not Power On or Intermittent Power Loss
description: Identifies causes of no power or intermittent shutdowns, including power supply, battery, and connection issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not in use with a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Check that the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device. Why: Loss of facility power is a common and easily overlooked cause.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the power cord is fully seated at both the wall and table connection point. Look for visible damage, fraying, or bent pins. If damage is found, remove from service.

steps.title: Check Power Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Observe for any LEDs or indicators on the base or control panel. No lights → possible power supply issue. Intermittent lights → possible loose connection or failing battery.

steps.title: Attempt Power On Using Multiple Controls
steps.instructions: Try powering on using: Main control panel and Hand pendant (if equipped). Why: Helps rule out a faulty control interface.

steps.title: Check Battery Status (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and attempt to power the table using battery only. Then reconnect AC power and observe charging indicators. Expected Outcome: No battery function → battery may be depleted or failed. Intermittent operation → battery may not be holding charge.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Compartment (External Check Only)
steps.instructions: If accessible without disassembly, confirm battery is properly seated. Look for corrosion or loose connections. Do not open sealed compartments.

steps.title: Cycle Power
steps.instructions: Disconnect from AC power for 30–60 seconds, then reconnect and attempt restart. Why: Resets minor control or power module faults.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Use Factors
steps.instructions: Ask staff if power loss occurs during movement or positioning. If yes → possible internal cable strain or loose connection. If random → possible power module or battery issue.

steps.title: Swap Power Cord (If Compatible and Available)
steps.instructions: Use a known good cord to rule out cord failure. Why: Damaged cords can cause intermittent power loss.

CCR.Complaint: Surgical table would not power on and intermittently shut off during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed internal battery not holding charge, causing intermittent power loss when AC connection fluctuated.
CCR.Resolution: Removed table from service and sent to vendor for battery replacement and power system evaluation.

title: Getinge Magnus Series Surgical Table - Hand Control or IR Remote Not Responding
description: Identifies causes of non-responsive hand control or IR remote, including connection, pairing, battery, and obstruction issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not in active use with a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Table Has Power
steps.instructions: Check that the table powers on using the main control panel or base controls. If the table does not power on, troubleshoot power first. If powered, continue.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable (if wired)
steps.instructions: Check for secure connection at the table port. Look for bent pins, frayed cable, or loose connection. Reseat the connector firmly. If damaged, replace the hand control.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect both hand control and IR remote for cracks, fluid ingress, or stuck buttons. Stuck or damaged buttons can prevent input recognition.

steps.title: Test Multiple Controls (if available)
steps.instructions: Use a secondary hand control or backup control if available. If alternate control works, original control is likely faulty.

steps.title: Check IR Remote Batteries
steps.instructions: Replace batteries in the IR remote with known good ones. Weak batteries are a common cause of non-response.

steps.title: Verify Line-of-Sight for IR Remote
steps.instructions: Ensure there is a clear path between the remote and the IR receiver on the table base. Remove any drapes, equipment, or obstructions. IR requires direct visibility to function.

steps.title: Clean IR Receiver Window
steps.instructions: Inspect and clean the IR receiver lens on the table. Dust or residue can block signal reception.

steps.title: Check for Interference
steps.instructions: Look for strong lighting sources or other IR-emitting devices nearby. Surgical lights or other IR devices may interfere with signal reception.

steps.title: Cycle Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Turn the table off and back on. This may reset communication between control devices and the table.

steps.title: Check for Lockout or Control Disable Settings
steps.instructions: Verify that the table is not in a locked or disabled control mode. Some configurations disable remote input for safety.

steps.title: Attempt Re-Pairing (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If the system uses wireless pairing, follow manufacturer process to re-sync the remote. If pairing fails, suspect control or receiver issue.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported hand control and IR remote not responding on Magnus surgical table.
CCR.Cause: IR remote batteries depleted and hand control cable found loose at connection point.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced batteries, reseated hand control connection, and verified full table control functionality.

helpfulDetails: Table powered on and responsive via base controls
Hand control cable condition and connection status

title: Getinge Magnus Series Surgical Table - Table Movement Functions Inoperative
description: Identifies causes of complete loss of table movement, including power, control input, lockout conditions, and connection issues before internal failure is assumed.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not actively supporting a patient. If in use, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Main Power Status
steps.instructions: Check that the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Confirm power indicator lights are present on the base. If no power indicators → suspect power supply or cord issue.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop / Lockout Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect for an engaged emergency stop or system lock. Some Magnus tables disable movement if safety lockouts are active. Reset if applicable and retest.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Charge Level
steps.instructions: If running on battery, verify the battery is charged. Connect to AC power and allow a few minutes, then retry movement. A depleted battery can prevent all motion functions.

steps.title: Test Multiple Control Inputs
steps.instructions: Attempt movement using hand control (pendant), IR remote (if equipped), and backup control panel on the table base. If one control works → issue is isolated to the non-working controller. If none work → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control / Pendant Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the pendant cable is fully seated and not damaged. Look for bent pins, frayed wiring, or loose connectors. Reseat connection and retest.

steps.title: Check for IR Remote Issues (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Verify batteries in the IR remote. Ensure there is a clear line of sight to the receiver. Confirm no obstruction or bright light interference.

steps.title: Listen for Motor or Relay Activity
steps.instructions: Attempt a movement command and listen. If motors or relays engage but no movement → possible mechanical or actuator issue. If no sound at all → likely control or power distribution issue.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Visually check for debris under the table, binding joints, or accessories interfering with movement. Remove obstruction if found and retest.

steps.title: Check System Status Indicators or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Observe for fault codes, blinking LEDs, or alarm tones. Document any error indications for further diagnosis or escalation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Table
steps.instructions: Turn the table off (if possible), disconnect from AC power, wait 30 seconds, then reconnect and power on. This can reset control system faults. If movement is restored → monitor for recurrence.

CCR.Complaint: Table not responding to any movement commands from hand control or remote.
CCR.Cause: Loose hand control connection at base prevented control signal transmission.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated hand control connector and verified full table movement functionality restored.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights or fault codes observed

title: Getinge Magnus Series Surgical Table - Table Drift or Inability to Hold Position
description: Identifies causes of table drifting or failing to hold position, including hydraulic, lock, load, and control issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not actively supporting a patient. If in use, transfer the patient to another stable surface before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify Table Lock Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the table base is fully locked. A partially engaged or unlocked base can allow unintended movement or drift. Expected Outcome: Table remains stable when locked.

steps.title: Check Floor Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect the floor for uneven surfaces, debris, or fluid contamination. An unstable surface can mimic drift or prevent proper locking. Expected Outcome: Table sits firmly without rocking or shifting.

steps.title: Confirm Weight Limits and Distribution
steps.instructions: Verify the patient weight and positioning are within manufacturer limits. Uneven or excessive load can cause hydraulic drift or inability to hold position. Expected Outcome: Properly distributed load improves stability.

steps.title: Inspect for External Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check for cables, accessories, or attachments interfering with movement or locking mechanisms. Accessories improperly mounted can affect balance or movement resistance. Expected Outcome: No obstruction impacting table positioning.

steps.title: Operate Table Through Full Range of Motion
steps.instructions: Using the hand control, move the table through several positions. Observe for slow settling or inability to maintain position after movement stops. Expected Outcome: Table holds position without gradual movement.

steps.title: Check for Audible Hydraulic Activity or Creep
steps.instructions: Listen for continued motor or hydraulic sounds after movement stops. This may indicate internal pressure loss or valve issues. Expected Outcome: No continued sound once movement stops.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Fluid Leaks
steps.instructions: Check around the base and column for signs of hydraulic fluid. Leaks can lead to pressure loss and drifting. Expected Outcome: No visible fluid or residue.

steps.title: Cycle Power to Reset Control System
steps.instructions: Power the table off, wait 30 seconds, and power back on. This can reset control logic that may affect holding functions. Expected Outcome: Normal holding function restored if control-related.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Control (if available)
steps.instructions: If equipped, try a secondary control (IR remote or backup pendant). Helps rule out unintended input or control malfunction. Expected Outcome: Consistent behavior across controls confirms non-control issue.

CCR.Complaint: Surgical staff reported table drifting downward during procedures.
CCR.Cause: No external issues found; suspected internal hydraulic pressure loss.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for vendor repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (removed from service or operational)

title: Getinge Magnus Series Surgical Table - Battery Not Charging or Short Runtime
description: Identifies causes of battery not charging or short runtime, including battery condition, charger function, connection, and load issues before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not in use with a patient. If it is, safely transfer the patient to another table before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the wall outlet is live using a known-good device. Ensure any extension cords or power strips are functional. Outcome: Table should detect incoming AC power.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Connections
steps.instructions: Turn off the table and disconnect power. Remove the battery and check terminals for corrosion, debris, or loose connections. Re-seat the battery securely. Outcome: Proper contact ensures charging can occur.

steps.title: Verify Charger Function
steps.instructions: Confirm charger is correctly connected to the table or battery. Check for indicator lights on the charger showing it is actively charging. Swap with a known-good charger if available. Outcome: Charger is confirmed operational or replaced.

steps.title: Check Battery Status and Runtime
steps.instructions: Observe battery level indicators on the table or hand control. If the table powers on but runtime is short, the battery may be degraded. Outcome: Determine if battery retains sufficient charge for normal use.

steps.title: Review Load and Usage Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure table is not under excessive load or using high-energy movements continuously, which can shorten runtime. Outcome: Confirm short runtime is not caused by operational overload.

CCR.Complaint: Table battery not charging and provides only short runtime.
CCR.Cause: Battery terminals were corroded, and battery capacity is below specification.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned terminals, tested charger, battery replaced, and table verified to hold full charge.

title: Getinge HS Series Washer-Disinfector - Cycle Abort Due to Water Temperature Failure
description: Identifies and resolves cycle aborts caused by water temperature deviations or heating issues, focusing on external and easily verifiable causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Verify the washer is empty of instruments and staff is clear of spray arms. Why: Prevents burns or accidental exposure during testing.

steps.title: Check Power Supply and Indicators
steps.instructions: Confirm unit is plugged into a live outlet and main power switch is ON. Inspect display for error codes related to heating or temperature. Expected: Display powers on and no mains voltage warnings.

steps.title: Verify Water Supply and Temperature Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure hot and cold water supply valves are fully open. Confirm incoming water temperature is within manufacturer-specified range (~20–50 °C). Expected: Steady inlet water flow without leaks.

steps.title: Inspect Detergent and Rinse Additive Systems
steps.instructions: Check that dosing pumps and lines are clear and operational. Why: Some cycles abort if dosing solutions affect sensor readings. Expected: Proper flow and no airlocks in supply lines.

steps.title: Check Temperature Sensors and Probes
steps.instructions: Ensure sensors are properly seated in their channels; inspect for visible damage. Expected: No loose probes; connectors seated securely.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle
steps.instructions: Initiate a short or test cycle without instruments. Observe temperature ramp-up and heating progress. Expected: Unit reaches set temperatures without abort.

steps.title: Swap or Reset External Components if Possible
steps.instructions: If a sensor or inlet valve has a redundant counterpart, swap temporarily to test. Expected: Cycle completes if external component was causing the issue.

CCR.Complaint: Washer aborted cycles with “water temperature error” displayed.
CCR.Cause: Inlet water supply verified; temperature sensors seated; issue persisted.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and sent for manufacturer repair.

title: Getinge HS Series Washer-Disinfector - Detergent Dosing Pump Not Priming
description: Addresses external causes of detergent pump failure, including blockages, air locks, and supply issues, ensuring proper dosing during cycles.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm washer is empty and staff are clear of spray. Why: Prevents accidental exposure to chemicals or moving parts.

steps.title: Verify Detergent Supply
steps.instructions: Check detergent container for adequate volume and correct type. Expected: Pump should have a proper supply to prime. Why: Empty or wrong detergent prevents priming.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Examine all suction and feed lines for kinks, leaks, or disconnections. Expected: Tubing free of obstructions and properly connected. Why: Blocked or loose tubing stops detergent from reaching pump.

steps.title: Check for Air Locks
steps.instructions: Remove air from lines by loosening connection at pump inlet slightly, then retighten. Expected: Air bubbles escape; pump can now prime. Why: Air trapped in the line prevents proper suction.

steps.title: Prime the Pump Manually (If Supported)
steps.instructions: Follow manufacturer instructions to manually prime or reset pump. Expected: Pump should start drawing detergent. Why: Some HS Series units require manual intervention after empty refill.

steps.title: Inspect Pump and Strainer
steps.instructions: Remove and clean inlet strainer; check for visible debris. Expected: Strainer clear; pump can draw fluid. Why: Debris may prevent suction.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle
steps.instructions: Start a short cycle with detergent dosing enabled. Expected: Pump primes and delivers detergent successfully. Why: Confirms issue resolved before returning to service.

CCR.Complaint: Washer failed to prime detergent pump during a standard cleaning cycle.
CCR.Cause: Tubing partially blocked and air trapped in inlet line; detergent supply adequate.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared tubing, removed air lock, and manually primed pump; test cycle successful.

title: Getinge HS Series Washer-Disinfector - Door Seal Leak
description: Identifies external causes of door seal leaks, including gasket wear, misalignment, and debris.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the washer is empty and staff are clear of potential spray.

steps.title: Inspect Door Seal / Gasket
steps.instructions: Open the door and visually check for cracks, tears, or hardening of the gasket.

steps.title: Check for Foreign Objects or Debris
steps.instructions: Wipe the seal and door frame to remove trapped debris, lint, or detergent residue.

steps.title: Verify Door Latch Engagement
steps.instructions: Close the door and ensure it fully engages and locks without excessive force.

steps.title: Check Alignment of Door and Chamber
steps.instructions: Look for uneven gaps between seal and chamber edge. Adjust door hinge if minor misalignment is noted.

steps.title: Perform a Test Fill Cycle
steps.instructions: Run a short rinse or fill cycle without instruments to observe for leaks.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported water leaking from washer door during cycle.
CCR.Cause: Gasket showed signs of wear and minor misalignment of door frame.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned debris and tested door; leak persisted, unit labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Getinge HS Series Washer-Disinfector - Spray Arm Blockage
description: Identifies external causes of spray arm blockage and ensures cleaning effectiveness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm washer is empty and staff are clear of potential spray. Why: Prevents injury from sudden water release or chemical exposure.

steps.title: Inspect Spray Arms for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Remove upper and lower spray arms. Check for visible debris, detergent buildup, or mineral deposits in nozzles. Expected outcome: Nozzles should be clear; debris can cause weak or uneven spray.

steps.title: Clean Spray Arms
steps.instructions: Rinse thoroughly under tap water; use a soft brush or toothpick to clear small holes. Expected outcome: Water flows freely through all spray holes.

steps.title: Check Spray Arm Rotation
steps.instructions: Manually rotate the spray arms to ensure they move freely without obstruction. Expected outcome: Spray arms rotate smoothly; restricted movement indicates blockage or mechanical interference.

steps.title: Inspect Connections and Fittings
steps.instructions: Verify spray arms are seated correctly on their mounts and that hoses or connectors are not kinked or loose. Expected outcome: Proper alignment ensures consistent water distribution.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle
steps.instructions: With washer empty, run a rinse cycle to observe spray arm rotation and water coverage. Expected outcome: Spray arms rotate fully and no unusual noises are present; water reaches all areas of the chamber.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported poor cleaning in the upper tray and uneven spray.
CCR.Cause: Upper spray arm partially blocked with detergent residue; rotation restricted.
CCR.Resolution: Spray arm removed, cleaned, and reinstalled; test cycle completed successfully.

title: Getinge HS Series Washer-Disinfector - Control Panel or Display Errors
description: Identifies external causes of control panel or display errors, such as power issues, loose connections, or software faults, preventing cycle interruptions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the washer is empty and staff are clear of any spray. Why: Avoids injury while interacting with the device.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the washer is properly plugged into a live, dedicated outlet. Test with another device if needed. Expected Outcome: Display powers on without flickering or loss of function.

steps.title: Inspect Main Power Switch and Fuses
steps.instructions: Ensure the main power switch is fully engaged and fuses or circuit breakers are intact. Expected Outcome: Device powers up consistently; no blown fuses.

steps.title: Examine External Connections
steps.instructions: Check for loose or damaged cables to the display, control board, or auxiliary panels. Expected Outcome: Secure connections restore normal panel function.

steps.title: Reset the Washer
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit by turning off, waiting 30 seconds, and turning on again. Expected Outcome: Minor software glitches or temporary errors are cleared.

steps.title: Check for Visible Error Codes
steps.instructions: Note any error codes displayed; reference the manufacturer’s external troubleshooting chart for guidance. Expected Outcome: Identifies if issue is user-resettable or requires repair.

steps.title: Evaluate Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the control panel is not exposed to water spray, excessive heat, or condensation. Expected Outcome: Environment-related glitches are mitigated.

CCR.Complaint: Control panel intermittently displays errors and does not respond to input.
CCR.Cause: Loose power connection at main input detected during inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Secured power cable and verified proper operation; panel functions normally.

title: Getinge 8666 / 8668 Series Washer-Disinfector - Cycle not completing or aborting mid-cycle
description: Troubleshooting cycle abort or incomplete cycle issues caused by door, water, drainage, or external system conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure no instruments needed for immediate patient care are dependent on this unit. Expected: Backup equipment/process in place. Why it matters: Delays in reprocessing can impact patient care readiness.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Start a cycle and observe where it stops or aborts. Note any displayed error codes or alarms. Expected: Cycle stops at a repeatable stage or shows an error. Why it matters: Identifying the failure point narrows troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Door Closure and Latching
steps.instructions: Inspect door seal and confirm door fully closes and latches securely. Expected: Door closes firmly with no gaps or resistance. Why it matters: Door interlock failure will stop or prevent cycle progression. If door not fully latched → adjust/clean seal and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Gasket and Seal Area
steps.instructions: Check for debris, damage, or buildup on the gasket. Expected: Clean, intact gasket with proper seal. Why it matters: Poor sealing can trigger aborts due to pressure or leak detection. If debris found → clean and retest.

steps.title: Verify Water Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm facility water supply is on and valves are fully open. Check for kinks in supply lines. Expected: Adequate water flow to the unit. Why it matters: Insufficient water supply will stop the cycle early. If supply issue found → correct and retest.

steps.title: Check Drain System
steps.instructions: Inspect drain hose and connection for kinks, clogs, or backflow issues. Expected: Free and unobstructed drainage. Why it matters: Drain issues commonly cause mid-cycle aborts. If blockage found → clear and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Spray Arms and Filters
steps.instructions: Open chamber and check spray arms for free rotation and filters for debris buildup. Expected: Spray arms rotate freely and filters are clean. Why it matters: Restricted flow can trigger pressure or circulation faults. If debris found → clean components and retest.

steps.title: Check Chemical Levels (Detergent/Rinse Aid)
steps.instructions: Verify chemical containers are not empty and lines are properly connected. Expected: Adequate chemical levels and proper flow. Why it matters: Some cycles will abort if dosing conditions are not met. If low/empty → replace and retest.

steps.title: Review Alarm Codes or Messages
steps.instructions: Document any error codes displayed during the abort. Expected: Identifiable alarm or fault message. Why it matters: Provides direction for targeted repair if issue persists.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 30 seconds, then power back on and restart a cycle. Expected: System resets and cycle completes normally. Why it matters: Clears temporary faults or control glitches. If cycle completes → issue resolved.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector cycle aborting mid-cycle with alarm.
CCR.Cause: Debris buildup found in chamber filter restricting water flow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned filter, verified spray arm movement, and confirmed successful cycle completion.

title: Getinge 8666 / 8668 Series Washer-Disinfector - Door Not Sealing or Latch Failure
description: Troubleshooting door sealing and latch failures preventing cycle start due to gasket, alignment, or latch issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure no instruments needed for immediate patient care are dependent on this unit. Expected: Backup reprocessing method available. Why it matters: Delays in instrument processing can impact patient care readiness.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to close the door and start a cycle. Observe for gaps, resistance, or latch failure. Expected: Door does not fully close, latch does not engage, or unit alarms. Why it matters: Confirms a true sealing or latching issue.

steps.title: Inspect Door Closure Path for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check door edges, hinges, and chamber opening for trays, racks, or debris blocking closure. Expected: Clear path with no obstruction. Why it matters: Even small obstructions prevent proper sealing and latch engagement. If obstruction is found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Check Door Gasket Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the door gasket for cracks, flattening, buildup, or displacement. Expected: Gasket is intact, clean, and evenly seated. Why it matters: A damaged or misaligned gasket prevents a proper seal. If dirty → clean gasket and sealing surface, then retest. If damaged → remove from service for replacement.

steps.title: Inspect Door Alignment and Fit
steps.instructions: Close the door slowly and observe alignment with the chamber frame. Expected: Door sits flush and evenly without shifting or binding. Why it matters: Misalignment prevents proper latch engagement and sealing. If misaligned → check hinges for looseness or wear.

steps.title: Check Door Latch Mechanism
steps.instructions: Operate the latch and observe for smooth engagement. Listen for clicking or locking action. Expected: Latch engages fully and holds door securely. Why it matters: Worn or stuck latches are a common cause of sealing failure. If stiff or sticking → inspect for debris and clean. If latch does not engage → likely mechanical failure.

steps.title: Inspect Latch Receiver/Strike Plate
steps.instructions: Check the strike plate or receiving mechanism inside the chamber frame. Expected: Proper alignment with latch and no visible damage. Why it matters: Misalignment or wear prevents latch from securing the door. If loose → tighten if accessible. If worn/damaged → remove from service.

steps.title: Check for Door Interlock or Sensor Feedback (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Observe if the unit recognizes the door as closed (no “door open” alarm). Expected: System acknowledges door closure. Why it matters: Sensor/interlock issues can mimic latch failure. If door appears closed but not recognized → possible interlock issue.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Heat or Mechanical Stress
steps.instructions: Check for warped door edges, discoloration, or uneven sealing surfaces. Expected: No warping or distortion. Why it matters: Repeated heat cycles can deform components and prevent sealing. If warping is present → remove from service.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector door not sealing and cycle will not start.
CCR.Cause: Worn door gasket and latch not fully engaging due to alignment shift.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned sealing surface, confirmed gasket wear, removed unit from service for gasket and latch repair.

helpfulDetails: Latch engagement behavior (clicking, loose, not engaging)
Final device status (in service or removed)

title: Getinge 8666 / 8668 Series Washer-Disinfector - Detergent Dosing Pump Failure
description: Troubleshooting detergent dosing failure causing poor cleaning results or alarms.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no critical instruments are dependent on this unit. Expected: Backup reprocessing method available. Why it matters: Cleaning failure directly impacts patient safety and instrument sterility.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe for detergent-related alarms or lack of chemical draw. Expected: No detergent usage, alarm, or visibly unchanged detergent level. Why it matters: Confirms a true dosing issue vs perceived cleaning concern.

steps.title: Check Detergent Supply Level
steps.instructions: Inspect the detergent container for adequate volume. Expected: Sufficient detergent present. Why it matters: Empty or low containers are the most common cause of dosing failure. If empty → replace/refill and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Pickup Tube and Container Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the suction tube is properly inserted into the detergent container and not kinked or disconnected. Expected: Tube fully submerged, no cracks or air gaps. Why it matters: Air leaks prevent proper suction and dosing. If issue found → reseat or replace tubing and retest.

steps.title: Check for Blockages in Tubing
steps.instructions: Visually inspect tubing for buildup, crystallization, or blockage. Expected: Clear, flexible tubing with no restrictions. Why it matters: Chemical residue can harden and block flow. If blockage found → replace tubing and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Pump Operation During Cycle
steps.instructions: Start a cycle and listen/observe for dosing pump activation. Expected: Audible or visible pump activity during dosing phase. Why it matters: Confirms whether the pump is receiving command and attempting to operate. If no activity → possible electrical or control issue.

steps.title: Check Chemical Filter (if equipped)
steps.instructions: Inspect inline chemical filters for clogging. Expected: Filter clear and allowing flow. Why it matters: A clogged filter restricts suction and mimics pump failure. If clogged → replace and retest.

steps.title: Verify Correct Chemical Container Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct detergent type is connected and container cap/vent is not sealed airtight. Expected: Proper chemical with vented container. Why it matters: Non-vented containers create vacuum lock preventing fluid draw.

steps.title: Inspect for Air in Line (Loss of Prime)
steps.instructions: Look for air gaps or empty tubing indicating loss of prime. Expected: Tubing filled with detergent. Why it matters: Dosing pumps require primed lines to function properly. If air present → prime system per facility procedure and retest.

steps.title: Check External Electrical Connections (if accessible)
steps.instructions: Verify pump wiring connections are secure without opening internal panels. Expected: No loose or visibly damaged external wiring. Why it matters: Loose connections can prevent pump activation.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector not dispensing detergent during cycle; poor cleaning observed.
CCR.Cause: Cracked suction tubing causing air leak and loss of detergent draw.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced suction tubing, primed system, and verified proper detergent dosing during test cycle.

helpfulDetails: Final operational status after test cycle

title: Getinge 8666 / 8668 Series Washer-Disinfector - Temperature not reaching setpoint
description: Cycle runs but fails to reach programmed temperature due to water supply, loading, sensor, or heating-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Operational Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no critical instruments are dependent on this unit. Expected: Backup reprocessing method available. Why it matters: Inadequate temperature compromises disinfection effectiveness and patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe temperature readings and any alarms. Expected: Temperature stalls below setpoint or alarms out. Why it matters: Confirms a true heating issue and identifies when failure occurs.

steps.title: Check Incoming Water Supply Temperature
steps.instructions: Verify facility hot water supply temperature to the unit. Expected: Incoming water meets manufacturer requirements (typically pre-heated). Why it matters: Low inlet temperature increases heating demand and may prevent reaching setpoint. If low → escalate to facilities and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Water Supply Flow and Pressure
steps.instructions: Ensure adequate water pressure and flow to the unit (no partially closed valves or restrictions). Expected: Strong, consistent water flow. Why it matters: Insufficient flow affects heating efficiency and circulation. If restricted → restore flow and retest.

steps.title: Check Chamber Loading and Rack Configuration
steps.instructions: Inspect load size and placement of trays/instruments. Expected: Proper loading without overloading or blocked spray arms. Why it matters: Overloading reduces heat distribution and prevents uniform temperature rise. If overloaded → adjust load and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Spray Arms for Rotation and Blockage
steps.instructions: Check that spray arms rotate freely and are not clogged. Expected: Free movement with clear nozzles. Why it matters: Poor circulation limits heat transfer throughout the chamber. If blocked → clean and retest.

steps.title: Verify Detergent and Chemical Dosing
steps.instructions: Confirm proper detergent dosing and correct chemical type. Expected: Proper dosing during cycle. Why it matters: Incorrect chemistry can affect heat transfer and cleaning performance. If dosing issue found → correct and retest.

steps.title: Check Door Seal Integrity
steps.instructions: Inspect door gasket for damage, debris, or improper sealing. Expected: Tight seal with no visible gaps. Why it matters: Heat loss from poor sealing prevents reaching temperature setpoint. If seal compromised → clean/adjust or remove from service if damaged.

steps.title: Observe for Heating Indicators or Alarms
steps.instructions: Monitor for heating-related alarms or lack of temperature increase during heating phase. Expected: Gradual and steady temperature rise. Why it matters: Lack of heating response suggests heater or control-related issue after external causes are ruled out.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector not reaching programmed temperature during cycle.
CCR.Cause: Incoming hot water temperature below required range due to partially closed supply valve.
CCR.Resolution: Restored water flow, verified inlet temperature, and confirmed unit reached setpoint during test cycle.

helpfulDetails: Any temperature alarms or codes observed

title: Getinge 8666 / 8668 Series Washer-Disinfector - Drain or Circulation Pump Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for washer-disinfector drain or circulation pump issues including flow restriction, blockage, and external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no critical instruments are dependent on this unit. Ensure a backup reprocessing method is available. Why it matters: Incomplete cleaning or drainage compromises instrument safety and patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe for standing water, lack of spray pressure, or pump-related alarms. Expected: Water not draining, weak/no spray, or abnormal noise. Why it matters: Confirms whether issue is drain-related, circulation-related, or both.

steps.title: Check Chamber for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the bottom of the chamber for debris (instrument parts, wrappers, sediment). Expected: Clear sump area with no visible blockage. Why it matters: Debris is a common cause of restricted flow affecting both drain and circulation. If debris is found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect and Clean Drain Filter/Strainer
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the drain filter or strainer for buildup. Expected: Filter clean and unobstructed. Why it matters: A clogged filter restricts water flow and can mimic pump failure. If clogged → clean thoroughly and retest.

steps.title: Check Spray Arms for Blockage or Restricted Movement
steps.instructions: Ensure spray arms spin freely and no jets are clogged. Expected: Free rotation and clear spray holes. Why it matters: Poor circulation may be mistaken for pump failure. If blocked → clean and retest.

steps.title: Verify Drain Hose and Facility Drain Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the drain hose for kinks, clogs, or improper routing. Check facility drain connection for backup or restriction. Expected: Unrestricted drainage path. Why it matters: External plumbing issues can prevent proper draining. If restriction found → correct and retest.

steps.title: Listen for Pump Operation
steps.instructions: During drain and wash phases, listen for pump activation (humming, vibration). Expected: Audible pump operation during appropriate cycle stages. Why it matters: Helps differentiate between electrical/control issues vs mechanical blockage. If pump runs but no flow → likely blockage or impeller issue. If pump silent → possible power/control or internal failure.

steps.title: Check for Airlocks or Improper Water Levels
steps.instructions: Ensure proper fill level and no trapped air in system (run another cycle if needed). Expected: Normal fill and consistent circulation. Why it matters: Improper water levels can affect pump priming and performance.

steps.title: Inspect for External Leaks or Signs of Pump Strain
steps.instructions: Check underneath and around unit for leaks, unusual noise, or overheating smell. Expected: No leaks, normal operating sound. Why it matters: Mechanical failure signs indicate internal pump damage. If abnormal noise, heat, or leaks are present → remove from service.

CCR.Complaint: Washer-disinfector not draining with standing water remaining after cycle.
CCR.Cause: Drain filter clogged with debris restricting water flow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned drain filter, verified proper drainage, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Presence of standing water after cycle

title: Getinge ED-Flow Endoscope Reprocessor (AER) - Cycle Not Starting or Aborting Mid-Process
description: Cycle fails to start, stops mid-process, or aborts due to door, supply, connection, or setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while a scope is needed for immediate patient care. Move to a backup AER or manual reprocessing if required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to start a cycle and observe behavior. Note any alarms or messages. Identify when the cycle stops (startup vs mid-cycle). Check if issue is consistent.

steps.title: Check Door Closure and Seal (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Ensure door is fully closed and latched. Inspect gasket for debris, damage, or misalignment. Reopen and firmly close door.

steps.title: Verify Water Supply and Pressure
steps.instructions: Ensure supply valves are open. Check for kinks in supply lines. Confirm facility water pressure is within normal range.

steps.title: Check Chemical Levels and Containers
steps.instructions: Verify containers are not empty. Ensure proper seating of chemical containers. Check for expired or incorrect chemicals.

steps.title: Inspect Scope Connections and Channel Hookups
steps.instructions: Verify all channel connectors are correctly attached. Confirm correct hookup for scope type. Check for loose or disconnected lines.

steps.title: Check for Blockages or Flow Restrictions
steps.instructions: Look for kinked or obstructed tubing. Check internal/external filters (if accessible). Ensure no visible debris in lines.

steps.title: Confirm Drain Function
steps.instructions: Check drain line for kinks or blockage. Ensure facility drain is not backing up.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn unit off. Wait 30–60 seconds. Restart and attempt cycle again.

CCR.Complaint: AER cycle not starting and aborting shortly after initiation.
CCR.Cause: Door not fully sealing due to debris on gasket preventing proper latch detection.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned gasket and reseated door; verified successful cycle completion.
